We need to figure out the problem: We need to explore rooms and build a graph representing map we have uncovered. At each step we choose a direction (north/south/east/west) based on available directions from current room. The problem description: "goal is to visit all the rooms with the fewest number of room changes possible." The map is unknown. We must decide direction to go and provide graph representation of map we have uncovered. Also need to recognize when there are no new rooms to visit and decide that we are done. Use format: {"action":"GO: DIRECTION","graph":{"nodes":[], "edges":{"north": [], "south": [], "east": [], "west": []}}} and replace DIRECTION with one of [north, south, east, west]. To stop the exploration, answer with "DONE" instead. Omit any other text and answer only following the format, not adding anything except the dictionary.

The format they gave: "edges":{"north": [], "south": [], "east": [], "west": []} - perhaps edges for each direction indicating connections discovered? In example they used edges with directional connections: "west": [("Living Room", "Library")] etc. They treat edges as mapping direction to list of pairs (from node to neighbor). In each graph state they include all nodes visited and edges discovered. For each direction, edges indicate discovered connections in that direction? Or edges per direction indicate connections that go in that direction from node to neighbor? Let's analyze example.

First example: Starting at Living Room, available directions: south, west.

They output:

{"action":"GO: west", "graph": {"nodes":["Living Room"],"edges":{"north":[],"south":[],"east":[],"west":[]}}}

So they haven't added an edge because they haven't moved yet? The edge for west is empty because they haven't discovered any edge? However they moved to Library after executing GO: west. They had "You have made a step and entered a Library. Currently available directions: east, north."

Then they output:

{"action":"GO: north", "graph":{"nodes":["Living Room", "Library"],"edges":{"north":[],"south":[],"east":[],"west":[("Living Room", "Library")]}}}

Wait they added edge in "west": [("Living Room", "Library")]. That suggests that "west" direction edge list contains connections discovered via moving west from some node (maybe from Living Room). Indeed we moved west from Living Room, so we discovered edge connecting Living Room to Library, and they store under "west". In the second step they didn't add edge for north because they haven't moved north yet. Next step: they go north from Library to Kitchen, and output graph with "north": [("Library", "Kitchen")] etc. They also keep previous edges: "west": [("Living Room", "Library")].

Thus edges dictionary is per direction indicating adjacency discovered when moving in that direction. The graph includes all nodes visited (rooms) so far, and edges per direction as a list of tuples (from_node, to_node). The "edges" also maybe need to include edges for both directions? Not necessarily; they store direction of movement when we discovered them. So in subsequent steps if later we move east from Kitchen to some other room (maybe Dining Room), we will add edge under "east": [("Kitchen", "Dining Room")]. This matches example. So they maintain a cumulative graph.

Thus in each output we need to provide the graph representation of all discovered nodes and edges thus far (including from all prior moves). Use the same format with nodes list and edges dictionary. Goal: Visit all rooms with fewest number of room changes possible (i.e., minimize total steps). We also need to stop when there are no new rooms to visit. Need to "recognize once there are no new rooms to visit and decide that we are done at that point."
